title: Life of a centurion.
scripture_ref: ‘’When Jesus had finished saying all this the people who were listening, he entered Capernaum. There a centurion’s servant, whom his master valued highly, was sick and about to die. The centurion heard of Jesus and sent some elders of the Jews to him, asking him to come and heal his servant. When they came to Jesus, they pleaded earnestly with him, “This man deserves to have you do this, because he loves our nation and has built our synagogue.” So Jesus went with them He was not far from the house when the centurion sent friends to say to him: “Lord, don’t trouble yourself, for I do not deserve to have you come under my roof. That is why I did not even consider myself worthy to come to you. But say the word, and my servant will be healed. For I myself am a man under authority, with soldiers under me. I tell this one, ‘Go,’ and he goes; and that one, ‘Come,’ and he comes. I say to my servant, ‘Do this,’ and he does it.” When Jesus heard this, he was amazed at him, and turning to the crowd following him, he said, “I tell you, I have not found such great faith even in Israel.” Then the men who had been sent, returned to the house and found the servant well.’’
devotional:
Luke 7 introduces us to a Roman Centurion. As you may know a Roman Centurion was someone who was in charge of 100 plus men. We know a number of facts from history about the life of a Centurion in Israel at this time.

During the New Testament era, a Roman centurion was a professional military officer commanding a platoon of troops called a “century.” This could be anywhere from nearly one hundred to several hundred men. Each Roman legion was composed of 5,000-6,000 men, divided into multiple cohorts, each cohort composed of multiple centuries. As a result, a legion could contain as many as sixty centurions. Their importance was based on seniority, with the senior centurion in a legion being in a position of great prestige.

The rank of centurion was the highest that an ordinary soldier could reach. Those who filled this office were career soldiers who had to be good leaders. 15 years service was required before a solider could become a centurion. The discipline and efficiency of the Roman military machine depended on them. According to one source, centurions “were often the most experienced and best-informed men in the army.”

This particular centurion could not have been part of the Roman army since there were no Roman forces in Galilee at the time; instead, he probably belonged to the royal troops of Herod Antipas. Rulers appointed by Rome and were expected to maintain an army and to provide Rome with military support when necessary. The average lifespan of a Roman Centurion is not well-documented. However, it is believed that it was around 25-30 years. This is due to the harsh conditions of military life, as well as the high mortality rate in battle. The ancient historian Polybius offers a list of qualifications looked for in centurions. They must be not so much "seekers after danger as men who can command, steady in action, and reliable; they ought not to be over anxious to rush into the fight; but when hard pressed they must be ready to hold their ground and die at their posts. This centurion, would have been a highly paid military man. An average soldier was paid 75 denari; a centurion 3,750-7,500 denari.

To the Jew, the centurion had three things against him: he was bitterly hated because he was non-Jewish, a Gentile; he was of the nation that had conquered Palestine, and he was of the armed and occupying force.

Interestingly, every time a centurion is mentioned in the New Testament it is with honour.

So to be a centurion was a tough, dangerous job, which would require a ‘’kill or be killed’ attitude. And yet the centurion we meet in our story did not really fit the status-quo, he did not comply with conventional perceptions and understandings of what a centurion should be.

The world needs people like this centurion, who are atypical because they have a dynamic relationship with God. We need to become people who do not fit into nice, neat tidy boxes. People who are positively distinctive, not odd, strange or awkward, but people who threaten the essence of a godless culture through love and grace. This front line solider had the courage to be different, he had the courage not to be ‘pigeon-holed.’ I wonder if we have inadvertently turned the church into a ‘sausage factory’ where we mass produce a ’Christian experience,’ that allows us to feel content with the bland of the status-quo.
prayer: Prayer, Father, May I be positively influence in my world.
html:
Life of a centurion.

‘’When Jesus had finished saying all this the people who were listening, he entered Capernaum. There a centurion’s servant, whom his master valued highly, was sick and about to die. The centurion heard of Jesus and sent some elders of the Jews to him, asking him to come and heal his servant. When they came to Jesus, they pleaded earnestly with him, “This man deserves to have you do this, because he loves our nation and has built our synagogue.” So Jesus went with them He was not far from the house when the centurion sent friends to say to him: “Lord, don’t trouble yourself, for I do not deserve to have you come under my roof. That is why I did not even consider myself worthy to come to you. But say the word, and my servant will be healed. For I myself am a man under authority, with soldiers under me. I tell this one, ‘Go,’ and he goes; and that one, ‘Come,’ and he comes. I say to my servant, ‘Do this,’ and he does it.” When Jesus heard this, he was amazed at him, and turning to the crowd following him, he said, “I tell you, I have not found such great faith even in Israel.” Then the men who had been sent, returned to the house and found the servant well.’’
B. Counterculture living requires atypical people.

Luke 7 introduces us to a Roman Centurion. As you may know a Roman Centurion was someone who was in charge of 100 plus men. We know a number of facts from history about the life of a Centurion in Israel at this time.

During the New Testament era, a Roman centurion was a professional military officer commanding a platoon of troops called a “century.” This could be anywhere from nearly one hundred to several hundred men. Each Roman legion was composed of 5,000-6,000 men, divided into multiple cohorts, each cohort composed of multiple centuries. As a result, a legion could contain as many as sixty centurions. Their importance was based on seniority, with the senior centurion in a legion being in a position of great prestige.

The rank of centurion was the highest that an ordinary soldier could reach. Those who filled this office were career soldiers who had to be good leaders. 15 years service was required before a solider could become a centurion. The discipline and efficiency of the Roman military machine depended on them. According to one source, centurions “were often the most experienced and best-informed men in the army.”

This particular centurion could not have been part of the Roman army since there were no Roman forces in Galilee at the time; instead, he probably belonged to the royal troops of Herod Antipas. Rulers appointed by Rome and were expected to maintain an army and to provide Rome with military support when necessary. The average lifespan of a Roman Centurion is not well-documented. However, it is believed that it was around 25-30 years. This is due to the harsh conditions of military life, as well as the high mortality rate in battle. The ancient historian Polybius offers a list of qualifications looked for in centurions. They must be not so much "seekers after danger as men who can command, steady in action, and reliable; they ought not to be over anxious to rush into the fight; but when hard pressed they must be ready to hold their ground and die at their posts. This centurion, would have been a highly paid military man. An average soldier was paid 75 denari; a centurion 3,750-7,500 denari.

To the Jew, the centurion had three things against him: he was bitterly hated because he was non-Jewish, a Gentile; he was of the nation that had conquered Palestine, and he was of the armed and occupying force.

Interestingly, every time a centurion is mentioned in the New Testament it is with honour.

So to be a centurion was a tough, dangerous job, which would require a ‘’kill or be killed’ attitude. And yet the centurion we meet in our story did not really fit the status-quo, he did not comply with conventional perceptions and understandings of what a centurion should be.

The world needs people like this centurion, who are atypical because they have a dynamic relationship with God. We need to become people who do not fit into nice, neat tidy boxes. People who are positively distinctive, not odd, strange or awkward, but people who threaten the essence of a godless culture through love and grace. This front line solider had the courage to be different, he had the courage not to be ‘pigeon-holed.’ I wonder if we have inadvertently turned the church into a ‘sausage factory’ where we mass produce a ’Christian experience,’ that allows us to feel content with the bland of the status-quo.

Prayer: Prayer, Father, May I be positively influence in my world.
Bible in a year reading plan: -Judges 3-5
